lead.headline: EU artificial-intelligence pact moves to enforcement phase
lead.deck: Brussels publishes the first compliance deadlines for general-purpose model providers; US firms face a 90-day window to register.
lead.body: The European Commission released the enforcement schedule for the AI Act on Friday, setting October 1, 2026 as the registration deadline for general-purpose AI providers with the new AI Office in Brussels. Penalties reach seven percent of global annual turnover for non-registration and three percent for late filings. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind have filed; Meta and xAI each requested 60-day extensions. The registry applies only to models trained above 10^25 floating-point operations, excluding most open-weights releases. Commission spokesperson Margrethe Vestager told reporters in Strasbourg that 41 filings had been received by Thursday midnight. A parallel US federal framework from Senator Maria Cantwell remains in the Commerce Committee with no markup scheduled. Enforcement actions begin January 15, 2027.

panels.lede: Open-weights coalition tops 200 signatories, including two Turing laureates
panels.body: A petition urging governments to preserve open-weights releases of frontier models reached 214 signatories by Friday morning, organisers told Reuters. Signers include former US Chief Technology Officer Megan Smith, Turing laureates Yoshua Bengio and Geoffrey Hinton, and researchers from 37 universities across 14 countries. The text asks regulators to exempt OSI-licensed model weights from the EU AI Act's 10^25 FLOPs registration threshold. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind declined to sign, each citing ongoing legal review; Meta signed at 9:40 AM CET. The Mozilla Foundation and Allen Institute coordinated the effort with $1.2 million from the Ford Foundation. Signatures close April 30.

panels.lede: Senate clears 68-31 short-term spending extension through June 30
panels.body: The Senate voted 68-31 Thursday night to extend federal funding through June 30, averting a shutdown that would have begun at midnight Friday. The continuing resolution preserves fiscal-year 2026 spending levels for most agencies but cuts $340 million from the State and Local Cybersecurity Grant Program, a 19 percent reduction from its 2025 appropriation. Twelve Republicans joined 56 Democrats in favor; 28 Republicans and three Democrats opposed. The House takes up the measure Saturday at 2:00 PM ET. The White House indicated the President will sign without public remarks on the cyber grant reduction. The National Association of State CIOs called the cut a blow to 52 state-level cyber offices.

panels.lede: Treasury 10-year yield closes at 4.31%, down 4 bp after retail-sales miss
panels.body: US 10-year Treasury yields closed Friday at 4.31 percent, down 4 basis points on the day and 11 on the week, after March retail sales rose 0.2 percent versus a 0.5 percent consensus. The S&P 500 closed at 5,421.18, down 0.4 percent; the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.8 percent to 17,204.33. The dollar index dropped to 102.11. Federal funds futures now imply a 64 percent probability of a 25-basis-point cut at the June 17 FOMC meeting, up from 51 percent Thursday. Morgan Stanley reported first-quarter net revenue of $15.1 billion and earnings per share of $2.08, beating consensus of $1.94; its wealth-management division added $94 billion in net new assets.

panels.lede: Tokyo summit ends without joint statement on rare-earth supply
panels.body: Trade ministers from seven Indo-Pacific economies — Japan, South Korea, Australia, India, Vietnam, the Philippines, and Indonesia — wrapped a two-day Tokyo summit Friday without the expected joint declaration on rare-earth diversification. Japan's METI cited disagreement over export-control language from Seoul and Canberra that would license outbound shipments above 500 metric tons. Minister Yasutoshi Nishimura said working-level talks continue through May 23 in Manila. The seven economies imported 73 percent of their rare-earth magnets from a single supplier in 2025. The $4.2 billion procurement framework was pushed to September's Quad meeting.

pull_quote: The registry is a starting line, not a finishing tape — models will change faster than filings can be updated. — Margrethe Vestager, quoted by Reuters

briefs.body: The OpenSSL project shipped version 3.3.1 Friday addressing CVE-2026-4082, a session-resumption flaw in TLS 1.3 that allowed reuse of expired tickets. Patch within seven days.
Kernel 6.14 reached release candidate 3 on Friday. The networking subsystem sees the largest delta at 412 commits, including a rewrite of the nftables expression engine by Pablo Neira Ayuso.
The European Space Agency confirmed Friday that the Comet Interceptor launch window has slipped from 2028 to early 2029 following delays to the Ariane 6 manifest. Mission cost rises to €178 million.
Perplexity AI and Groq jointly disclosed $340 million in Series B funding Friday. Both cited H100 cluster availability as the primary bottleneck on inference capacity expansion through Q3.

A consortium of the Raleigh News & Observer, Charlotte Observer, and Kentucky Lantern published a six-month investigation Friday into private-equity ownership of 47 rural hospitals across four states.
GitHub announced Friday that copilot-agent enterprise tenants will receive SOC 2 Type II attestation by June 30. Audit scope covers code-suggestion logging and customer data isolation.

data_point.context: Training-compute threshold above which an AI model qualifies as 'general-purpose' under the EU AI Act, triggering registration with the AI Office in Brussels. Roughly the scale of GPT-4 at its 2023 release and Claude 3 Opus in 2024.

on_this_day.body: At 5:12 AM local time on April 18, 1906, a magnitude 7.9 earthquake struck along 296 miles of the San Andreas Fault. The quake and the three-day fire that followed destroyed 28,188 buildings across 490 city blocks. Estimates place the death toll at roughly 3,000 and displaced residents at 225,000, more than half the city's population at the time.
